particle_size_range: 1-130 μm (operational range; screening resolution 1-2 μm at 400× magnification; persorption up to 130 μm mentioned)
key_metrics_output: Particle presence/absence, morphology, size, birefringence, polymer type (polypropylene, polyvinylchloride identified via Py-GCMS), fiber identification (viscose), exclusion of procedural contaminants
abstract: This study develops and validates a tiered screening framework for identifying exogenous particles and suspect microplastics in situ in pathological tissue sections, using ileal tissue from 101 subjects who underwent endoscopy. Polarized light microscopy (PLM) was used to screen for birefringent particles, followed by exclusion criteria to distinguish true particles from procedural contaminants, and pathologist review. Of 35 samples with subepithelial particles, 91% were attributed to procedural contamination. True observations included starch particles likely entering via persorption and a single viscose fiber in the intestinal lumen. Supplemental pyrolysis gas chromatography mass spectrometry (Py-GCMS) analysis of colon tissue identified polypropylene and polyvinylchloride signatures. The framework demonstrates that while microplastic frequency in subepithelial mucosa was low, particles can penetrate the intestinal epithelium, and highlights the importance of rigorous screening to distinguish true tissue-resident particles from analytical artifacts.

abstract: screening framework to determine whether particles including microplastics can access tissues using human ileal tissue sections
of the 35 samples containing sub-epithelial particles, 31 (i.e., 91% of observations) were concluded to be due to procedural contamination
some of these particles, which include starch, likely entered the tissue via persorption
